title: Resource-efficient parallel entanglement generation for multinode quantum networks via time-bin multiplexing
authors: Wenbo Zhang, Jing Zheng, Yimin Wang, Tao Li
abstract: Nonlocal entanglement generation among multiple remote quantum nodes provides a critical foundation for a variety of counterintuitive quantum applications. The exponential loss of photons transmitting over optical fibers sets an upper limit for entangling these quantum nodes. Here, we propose a resource-efficient and parallel protocol for entangling multiple remote quantum nodes via time-bin multiplexing. The transmission of a single photon with qudit-encoding in the time-bin mode enables entangling multiple stationary qubits in parallel, when single photons and individual stationary qubits interfaces are used and photon-state modulations are properly introduced before subsequently impinging the photon into each interface. Our protocol can generate parallel multipartite entanglement among ($N\geq3$) quantum nodes with the dimension of the photonic time bins independent of $N$, exponentially reducing the requirements for the coherence time of the stationary qubits and for the complexity of the photonic modulations. These distinct features make our protocol particularly advantageous for the development of multinode quantum networks.
summary: This paper proposes a resource-efficient protocol for generating multiple GHZ states in parallel across a quantum network using time-bin multiplexing. By using a single photon to entangle multiple nodes simultaneously, the method reduces the complexity of optical modulations and the necessary coherence time for stationary qubits. This approach offers a scalable pathway for building large-scale multinode quantum networks.
main_problem: Generating multipartite entanglement across multiple remote quantum nodes is limited by exponential photon loss in optical fibers and requires high coherence times and complex control.
main_result: The proposed protocol enables parallel generation of M GHZ states among N nodes using time-bin multiplexing, significantly reducing the required coherence time for stationary qubits and the complexity of photonic modulations.
method: A time-bin qudit-encoding protocol where a single photon interacts with cavity-qubit units via optical switches, inducing controlled-phase flips, followed by measurement in a generalized X-basis.
model_or_system: A network of N spatially separated nodes, each containing M stationary qubits coupled to single-sided cavities, modeled using a four-level atom system and cavity-QED dynamics.
key_observables: Average fidelity (F_M,N) and average efficiency (eta_M,N) of the generated GHZ states.
important_parameters: Node distance (L0), photon qudit dimension (d=2M), cooperativity (Cs), detuning (Delta_s), and qubit coherence times (T1, T2).
main_assumptions: The dimension of the photonic qudit is strictly tied to the number of parallel GHZ states being generated (d=2M).
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of the protocol for M=2 and N=3, illustrating the photon's path through nodes A, B, and C and its interaction with cavity-qubit units.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of scaling quantum networks, proposes a time-bin multiplexing protocol, describes the physical model and interaction mechanism, presents numerical results for fidelity and efficiency, and compares the protocol's advantages against conventional schemes.
why_interesting: It presents a scalable architecture for quantum networks that drastically relaxes the stringent requirements on qubit coherence and experimental control, which is highly relevant for developing practical quantum repeaters and distributed quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the compact protocol for generating two three- qubit GHZ states in parallel. Each node has two cavity-qubit units, each controlled by an optical switch to control the photon-qubit inter- action. The photonic time-bin qudit interacts probabilistically with the qubits in node A, and then travels to nodes B and C, where it in- teracts with the qubits in the same manner. At the final node, the pho- ton is measured by an X-basis detector, and upon successful detec- tion, the measurement outcome is communicated to the other nodes, thereby enabling the generation of two three-qubit GHZ states across three nodes.
